section.title: Warning Against Antichrists
section.sectionRationale: John turns from the world's pull to the sharpest danger inside the church: antichrists who deny that Jesus is the Christ. He explains that they went out from the fellowship because they never truly belonged, names the lie as denial of the Son, and reassures believers that the anointing they have keeps them in the truth.
section.thesis: In the last hour many antichrists deny that Jesus is the Christ and so deny the Father and the Son, but believers, taught and secured by the anointing they have received, must let the truth they heard from the beginning remain in them and so remain in the Son and the Father, who has promised them eternal life.
section.frameNote: Four beats: the last hour and the many antichrists; the lie that denies the Son and the Father; the call to let the truth remain and so remain in Christ; and the anointing that teaches and secures believers.

units.label: The last hour and the antichrists
units.note: those who went out from us

verses.text: Children, it is the last hour; and just as you have heard that the {{antichristos|antichrist}} is coming, so now many antichrists have appeared. This is how we know it is the last hour.
They went out from us, but they did not belong to us. For if they had belonged to us, they would have remained with us. But their departure made it clear that none of them belonged to us.

units.label: The lie that denies the Son

verses.text: You, however, have an {{chrisma|anointing}} from the Holy One, and all of you know the truth.
I have not written to you because you lack knowledge of the truth, but because you have it, and because no lie comes from the truth.
Who is the liar, if it is not the one who {{arneomai|denies}} that Jesus is the Christ? This is the antichrist, who denies the Father and the Son.
Whoever denies the Son does not have the Father, but whoever confesses the Son has the Father as well.

units.label: Let the truth remain in you

verses.text: As for you, let what you have heard from the beginning {{meno|remain}} in you. If it does, you will also remain in the Son and in the Father.
And this is the promise that He Himself made to us: eternal life.
I have written these things to you about those who are trying to {{planao|deceive}} you.

And as for you, the anointing you received from Him remains in you, and you do not need anyone to teach you. But just as His true and genuine anointing teaches you about all things, so remain in Him as you have been taught.

antichristos.gloss: Antichrist, one who stands against and in place of Christ. John uses it both for a coming figure his readers had heard about and for the many present deniers of Jesus already at work. To reject that Jesus is the Christ, the Son, is to embody the very spirit of antichrist.

chrisma.gloss: Anointing, that which is smeared or applied, here the anointing believers have received from the Holy One. It is closely tied to the Holy Spirit and the truth taught by Him. The word plays on Christ, the Anointed One: those who belong to the Anointed share His anointing and so know the truth.

arneomai.gloss: To deny, disown, refuse, renounce. The liar denies that Jesus is the Christ, disowning the Son. This is not honest doubt but a settled repudiation of the truth about Jesus, and John says it severs a person from the Father as well as the Son.

meno.gloss: To remain, abide, continue, dwell. John's key word for lasting fellowship. If the truth heard from the beginning remains in the believer, the believer remains in the Son and the Father. Abiding in the message and abiding in God are bound together; to hold the truth is to hold Him.

planao.gloss: To lead astray, deceive, cause to wander. The false teachers are actively trying to make believers wander from the truth about Christ. The word pictures being led off the path; John writes to keep his readers from being pulled away by these deceivers.

basic.context: John warns that it is the last hour, the stretch of time between Christ's first and second comings, and a sign of it is the appearing of many antichrists. These were teachers who had been part of the church but left, showing they never truly belonged. Their lie is denying that Jesus is the Christ, and John says that whoever denies the Son loses the Father too. But he reassures believers: they have an anointing from God that keeps them in the truth, so they must let what they first heard remain in them.
basic.hermeneutics: The passage teaches both discernment and assurance. It defines the antichrist not mainly as a future world ruler but as anyone who denies that Jesus is the Christ, the Son. And it comforts believers that they are not defenceless: the Spirit's anointing teaches and secures them. Verse 27, that you do not need anyone to teach you, is not against all teaching but against the deceivers, since John himself is teaching them.
basic.originalLanguages: It is the last hour, and many an antichristos, antichrist, has appeared. Believers have a chrisma, an anointing, from the Holy One, so they know the truth. The liar is the one who denies, arneomai, that Jesus is the Christ. John urges them to let the truth remain, meno, in them and so remain in God, and he writes because deceivers are trying to lead them astray, planao.
basic.typology: The coming of antichrists in the last hour fulfils the pattern of false prophets who arose among God's people, now intensified as the age of the Messiah dawns. The anointing believers receive answers the anointing of priests, kings, and prophets, now given to all God's people by the Spirit.
basic.christ: The whole test centres on Jesus: to confess that He is the Christ, the Son, is to have the Father; to deny Him is to lose the Father. Christ is the content of the faith once heard, the one in whom believers remain, and the giver of the promise, eternal life.
basic.god: God the Father cannot be had apart from the Son; to deny the Son is to deny the Father. God secures His people by His Spirit's anointing and by the truth He first gave them, and He has bound Himself by promise to give them eternal life.
basic.crossReferences: Matthew 24 warns of false christs and false prophets in the last days. John 16:13 promises the Spirit who guides into all truth. John 14:6 and John 17:3 tie knowing the Father to the Son and to eternal life.
basic.oneStory: As the age of Messiah dawns, the conflict between truth and lie sharpens, and deceivers deny the very Christ the story has been building toward. Yet God keeps His people by His Spirit and His word, so that those who hold to the Son hold the Father, and receive the eternal life promised through the whole of Scripture.

context.literary: The section is framed by warnings about deceivers, verses 18 and 26, and centres on the confession of Christ. It alternates between describing the antichrists and reassuring the readers, using 'You, however' and 'As for you' to mark the sharp contrast between the deceivers and the true believers.
context.historical: A group had seceded from the churches, denying that Jesus is the Christ come in the flesh, ideas linked to early docetic and proto-gnostic teaching. Their departure caused shock and doubt; John explains it and steadies the believers with the assurance of the Spirit's anointing.
context.canonical: The passage develops the New Testament expectation of false teachers and antichrists in the last days, Matthew 24, 2 Thessalonians 2, and joins the promise of the Spirit who teaches and guides into truth in John's Gospel, all centred on the confession that Jesus is the Christ, the Son of God.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To help the readers understand and withstand the departure of false teachers, to define the antichrist as the one who denies that Jesus is the Christ, and to assure believers that the Spirit's anointing and the truth they first received keep them in the Son and the Father.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: Verses 18 to 23 largely describe the antichrists and the lie they tell; verses 24 to 27 prescribe the response, let the truth remain in you, remain in Him. The promise of eternal life, verse 25, is a standing assurance for all who confess the Son.

debates.question: What does John mean when he says 'you do not need anyone to teach you' in verse 27?

views.label: You do not need the false teachers' new teaching, for the Spirit confirms the truth you already have
views.case: John is writing to warn against deceivers who claimed superior, secret knowledge. His point is that believers, having the Spirit's anointing and the apostolic message from the beginning, do not need these teachers to add anything or lead them into new doctrine. That John is himself teaching them, and that the New Testament everywhere values gifted teachers, shows he is not banning all instruction. This is the strongest reading and it fits the context of guarding against deceivers.

views.label: Believers need no human teachers at all, only the Spirit
views.case: Taken absolutely, the verse could seem to make all teaching unnecessary; but this contradicts John's own act of teaching and the New Testament's provision of teachers, Ephesians 4:11.

views.label: The anointing replaces the need to test any teaching
views.case: Some infer that the Spirit's anointing makes discernment automatic; but John urges active vigilance and testing, 1 John 4:1, so this overreads the verse.

debates.bearing: The strongest orthodox reading is that believers, indwelt by the Spirit and holding the apostolic truth, do not need the deceivers' novelties; the Spirit confirms and illumines the truth once delivered. This honours the Spirit's teaching while affirming the ongoing gift of faithful human teachers and the call to discernment.

debates.question: Who or what is the antichrist in this passage?

views.label: Both a coming figure and the many present deniers of Christ
views.case: John acknowledges his readers had heard that 'the antichrist is coming', a future figure, yet says 'many antichrists have appeared' now. He defines the antichrist essentially as anyone who denies that Jesus is the Christ, the Son. This reading holds together the expectation of a climactic opponent and the present reality of many deniers embodying that spirit. It is the most faithful to the text.

views.label: Only a single future world ruler
views.case: Some focus entirely on a final antichrist to come; but John's stress here is on the many antichrists already at work denying the Son.

views.label: Merely a symbol with no reality behind it
views.case: Others treat antichrist as a mere metaphor for error; but John names real people who left the church and a real denial of Christ.

debates.bearing: The strongest reading takes seriously both the expected climactic opponent and, John's chief concern, the many present antichrists who deny that Jesus is the Christ. The decisive test in every age is the confession of the Son, on which having the Father depends.

study.originalLanguages: John writes in the light of the last hour, when many an antichristos, one against and in place of Christ, has appeared. Against them believers have a chrisma, an anointing from the Holy One, a word that echoes Christ, the Anointed One: those who belong to the Anointed share His anointing and so know the truth. The liar is the one who denies, arneomai, disowns, that Jesus is the Christ, and that denial cuts a person off from the Father as well as the Son. So John's charge is that the truth heard from the beginning must meno, remain, in them, and then they will remain in the Son and the Father, guarded against the deceivers who are trying to planao, to lead them astray off the path.

typology.type: The antichrists rising in the last hour
typology.fulfillment: The pattern of false prophets among God's people, intensified as Messiah's age dawns and finally embodied in the climactic opponent Christ will destroy, 2 Thessalonians 2:8.

typology.type: The anointing given to all believers
typology.fulfillment: The anointing of priests, kings, and prophets, now poured out on all God's people by the Spirit, who teaches them the truth, John 16:13.

study.christ: Everything turns on the confession of Jesus as the Christ, the Son. To confess Him is to have the Father; to deny Him is to lose the Father too, for the Father is known only in the Son. Christ is the truth heard from the beginning, the one in whom believers remain, and the giver of the promise John names, eternal life. The anointing itself points to Him, the Anointed One, whose Spirit secures His people in the truth about Him.
study.god: God the Father will not be had apart from the Son; the two cannot be separated, so to reject Jesus is to reject the Father. Yet God is not passive in the face of deceivers. He secures His people by the anointing of His Spirit and by the word He first gave them, and He has bound Himself by promise to give them eternal life. He is a God who both warns His children plainly and keeps them faithfully.

crossReferences.note: Jesus warns of false christs and false prophets who would deceive, the pattern John sees in the many antichrists.
The Spirit of truth guides believers into all truth, the anointing that teaches John describes.
No one comes to the Father except through the Son, matching that denying the Son forfeits the Father.
Whoever runs ahead beyond the teaching of Christ does not have God, the same test of remaining in the truth about the Son.

study.oneStory: As the age of the Messiah dawns, the ancient conflict between truth and lie comes to a head, and deceivers deny the very Christ toward whom the whole story has moved. But God keeps His people by His Spirit's anointing and by the word first delivered to them, so that those who hold the Son hold the Father, and inherit the eternal life promised across all Scripture. To remain in the truth about Jesus is to remain in the God of the story, and in the life He has pledged.

sermon.title: A Word of Warning
sermon.why: Begg preaches this passage, 1 John 2:18-27, on the last hour, the antichrists who deny the Son, and the anointing that keeps believers in the truth. A clear, orthodox handling of discernment and assurance.

quiz.q: How does John explain the false teachers leaving the church?

options.text: They went out because they never truly belonged to us.
options.note: Verse 19: their departure showed they were never really of the fellowship.

options.text: They left because the church treated them unfairly.
options.note: John locates the cause in their not belonging, not in mistreatment.

options.text: They were true believers who simply lost interest.
options.note: John says plainly they did not belong to us; their leaving revealed it.

options.text: They moved on to plant other faithful churches.
options.note: They were deceivers, not faithful workers; their departure exposed them.

quiz.q: According to John, who is the liar and the antichrist?

options.text: The one who denies that Jesus is the Christ, thereby denying the Father and the Son.
options.note: Verse 22 defines the antichrist by denial of Jesus as the Christ.

options.text: Anyone who disagrees about minor matters of practice.
options.note: The issue is the central confession of Christ, not minor differences.

options.text: Only a single future world ruler yet to appear.
options.note: John does mention a coming antichrist, but here he stresses the many present deniers of the Son.

options.text: Anyone outside the church who has never believed.
options.note: John speaks of deceivers who denied Christ, not of unbelievers in general.

quiz.q: What does John say about the relationship between the Son and the Father in verse 23?

options.text: Whoever denies the Son does not have the Father; whoever confesses the Son has the Father too.
options.note: The Father cannot be had apart from the Son.

options.text: You can have the Father without the Son if you are sincere.
options.note: John says the opposite; denying the Son forfeits the Father.

options.text: The Son matters less than the Father.
options.note: John binds them together; to have one is to have the other.

options.text: The Father and Son are the same person with no distinction.
options.note: John distinguishes Father and Son while binding them together; this blurs that distinction.

quiz.q: What does John mean that believers 'do not need anyone to teach you' (verse 27)?

options.text: They do not need the deceivers' new teaching, for the Spirit confirms the truth they already have.
options.note: The point guards against false teachers, not against all faithful teaching, which John himself gives.

options.text: Believers need no human teachers of any kind, only the Spirit.
options.note: The Spirit does teach believers directly, but the verse does not rule out all human teachers, as John's own teaching shows.

options.text: The Spirit's anointing removes any need to test teaching.
options.note: John urges testing the spirits in 4:1; the anointing does not cancel discernment.

options.text: Believers should reject Scripture and follow feelings.
options.note: John points them to the truth heard from the beginning, not to feelings against Scripture.

quiz.q: What is the promise John highlights for those who remain in the truth?

options.text: Eternal life, the promise Christ Himself made.
options.note: Verse 25 names eternal life as the promise for those who remain in the Son.

options.text: A trouble-free life in this world.
options.note: John promises eternal life, not freedom from trouble now.

options.text: Special status above other believers.
options.note: The promise is eternal life for all who confess the Son, not elite status.

options.text: Complete understanding of every mystery now.
options.note: The Spirit teaches them the truth, but the promise named is eternal life, not exhaustive knowledge now.

questions: John says the departure of the false teachers showed they never truly belonged. How does this help you think about people who once professed faith and then walked away from Christ?
The antichrist is defined by denying that Jesus is the Christ. Where do you encounter subtle denials of who Jesus is today, and how do you recognise them?
Believers have an anointing that teaches them the truth. How have you experienced the Spirit confirming the truth about Jesus and steadying you against error?
John urges that what you heard from the beginning must remain in you. What practices help the core truths of the gospel stay lodged in your heart over the long haul?
To have the Son is to have the Father. How does the promise of eternal life for those who hold to Christ shape the way you face doubt, pressure, or deception?

translationNotes.note: 'The last hour' is the whole era between Christ's first and second comings, not a prediction of an immediate end; the many antichrists are a sign that this final age has arrived.

'All of you know the truth' follows a manuscript reading; the BSB footnote notes a variant, 'you know all things', in some texts. The assurance that believers are taught by the anointing is unchanged.
'You do not need anyone to teach you' is set against the deceivers offering new teaching; it does not forbid faithful instruction, which John is himself giving throughout the letter.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for 1 John 2:18-27. No em dashes in this passage. Variant at 2:20 ('you know all things') noted per the BSB footnote.
